videos.title: How to recognize Japan in GeoGuessr

hints.text: Land terms to learn: 4 main **islands** (Hokkaido, Honshu, Shikoku, Kyushu) → **regions** (prefecture groups) → **47 prefectures**
Google car black-and-white (rarely black / with antenna) + **low-cam**: camera set low — bigger blur below, road looks wide, low perspective

shared_with.note: The only other country that ALWAYS has low-cam.
shared_with.diff: In Switzerland the **car itself is blurred**; landscape/language obviously differ.

shared_with.note: Gen4 Sri Lanka also uses low-cam.
shared_with.diff: Sinhala script + tropical island → Sri Lanka.

hints.text: Drives on the **left**

shared_with.diff: Traditional characters + English + dense high-rises → HK.
Right-hand traffic + Hangul (circles/ovals in script) → Korea.
Right-hand traffic + traditional characters + scooter armies → Taiwan.

hints.text: Writing mixes **kanji** (Chinese-borrowed) + **hiragana** (simple, loopy — loops don't exist in Chinese) + **katakana** (geometric, for loan words/ads)

shared_with.diff: Any **hiragana loops** (の, し, つ) → Japan. Mainland China is tripod-only coverage.
Hiragana → Japan; HK adds English everywhere.

hints.text: Frequent **kanji in place names** + endings: 県 prefecture, 市 city, 区 ward, 町 town; cardinal directions 北 N, 南 S, 東 E, 西 W — learnable, speeds up finding cities
Architecture very distinct & varied: traditional country houses, regional designs, modern cities, reinforced coastal buildings
Round concrete poles with **screw-like extensions**; carry electrical plates, guy-wires, reflectors → key region-guessing assets
**Pole reflectors**: short, don't touch the ground — yellow vertical/diagonal or white stripes

shared_with.note: Taiwanese reflectors have diagonal stripes that **reach the ground**.

hints.text: Plates short **white with green text**; yellow/black = **Kei cars** (light automobiles)
Road lines: white outer + dashed white middle (most common) / solid yellow middle / yellow+white middle — **never yellow outer lines**
Simple **white bollards with circular reflector(s)** on top (sometimes black or with extensions; may carry city/prefecture kanji)
Simple **white guardrails** standard
Prefecture roads = **hexagonal shields** (prefecture kanji under the number; same number exists in several prefectures → find the prefecture first)
Common infra: unique **inverted-triangle stop sign**, school pedestrian crossing sign, **traffic mirrors** everywhere
**Retaining walls** — excellent Japan clue in information-poor rural mountains
**Area codes increase to the south** (codes ending in 0, esp. 0120, are non-regional)
Per-region **pole plates**, regional **reflector pads**, prefecture **road diamonds**, prefecture **signpost stickers**, regional **guy-wires** — the five core pole/sign metas (maps)
**Pole attachments by region**: Chugoku = orange arrow above top plate; Shikoku = orange-black bands + small 90° attachments; Kansai = ~120° attachments (or angled-down)
**Transformer metas by region**
bullets: **Hokkaido**: plus-shaped side bar or 1 transformer on two bars; several = sides of a square; never a wooden plate
**Tohoku**: square holding brackets + Tohoku Electric logo; round plate w/ zigzag bar; square w/ bar on TOP (Niigata uses this design too)
**Kanto**: mounted on a **wooden plate** + two bars, or round plate + T-bar
**Hokuriku**: thin metal bars, small 'lamp' insulators, no bar
**Chubu**: yellow painted numbers + black 'tap' connectors; big triangle+horizontal bars or thin brackets
**Kansai**: square with bar on the BOTTOM (vs Tohoku top)
**Chugoku**: cross bar mid-pole on a FULL triangle; white sticker w/ red numbers
**Shikoku**: cross bar on a HALF triangle, hat-shaped insulators; sticker w/ black numbers
**Kyushu**: straight side bar w/ 3 insulators; transformers 180° from each other
**Okinawa**: T-shaped side bar; transformers on opposite pole sides

hints.text: Grey **insulator-like blocks** (wires fed through) → Chubu, Kansai, Chugoku, Shikoku (very rarely Hokkaido)
**Pole tops**: 4 thick bars + circle → Chubu; filled 'tent' → Hokuriku; 4 thin bars → Chugoku; tuning fork w/ long top bar → Kanto; short top bar → Chubu; thin wire → Kansai
**Snow shoulder arrows** hang over roads (almost exclusive here)
bullets: Aomori variant: silver bolts around the border (rarely Hokkaido)
Gifu variant: long & thin
NH275 north: yellow-black design; SW of Sapporo: yellow-green

hints.text: **Bollard metas by prefecture/region**
bullets: **Kyushu + Yamaguchi**: flat white, orange reflectors on ONE side (Hokkaido rare variant = both sides)
**Shikoku**: black stripe under the reflector
**Tohoku**: thick white + octagonal reflector + yellow stripe (black variants on national highways); striped covering bollards esp. **Akita**
**Akita**: 2 yellow stripes under octagonal reflector
**Toyama**: extendable, 1 reflector + RED stripe (2 reflectors or yellow stripe = generic!)
**Okayama**: octagonal reflector + red stripe + sloped base — exclusive
**Aomori**: extendable, 2 yellow stripes + circular reflector
**N-Chugoku**: basic thin snow poles (also Iwate)

hints.text: **Guardrail metas**: green bands → Oita/Miyazaki (all Kyushu); yellow guardrails → Yamaguchi; chevron-like stickers → Hokkaido; red-yellow end stickers → Chugoku except Yamaguchi (rarely Niigata)
**Vertical traffic lights** → Hokkaido + northwestern coast (horizontal everywhere else)
Black signs with **two yellow circles** (also stacked or with arrows)
**Kerosene 'Claymore' tanks** + hot-water/propane tanks → more common the further north (abundant Hokkaido) & in high mountains; esp. rural towns
**Shosetsu** snow-melting roads (tiny circles in the pavement spraying warm water) → mainly northwest coastal prefectures
Cycling routes: **white outer + blue inner line** (mostly coastal; rare exceptions elsewhere)
**Red-tiled roofs** → Chugoku (esp. Hiroshima & Shimane); roofs get flatter/sloped further **north** (snow) + side ladders; **Okinawa** = white concrete flat-roof buildings
**Giant butterbur ('cabbage')** → abundant in Hokkaido + northern prefectures (smaller leaves further south)
bullets: Also on Sakhalin (Russia) — but no low-cam there

hints.text: **Crop metas**: green tea in hedged rows + frost fans → Shizuoka (36%) & Kagoshima (34%); rice → north of Tokyo (Tohoku 27%, Chubu 21%, Kanto 15%, Hokkaido 7%); sugarcane → S-Kyushu islands + Okinawa
**Vegetation metas**: Japanese white birch → Chubu & Hokkaido (some Tohoku; beech has grey spotted bark); bamboo → more south; Chinese silver grass → Kyushu (+Niigata/Yamagata/Akita)
Very limited **winter/snow coverage** — don't guess Hokkaido without certainty; mapped regions most likely
Very mountainous country — the recognisable flat exceptions: **Nobi & Kanto plains**
**Shisa guardian lions** + house plaques with specific characters; red-soil fields on Miyakojima & Ishigaki
**Mount Fuji** — tallest, gradual incline, snowy summit, visible from far → direction+distance = strong region guess
Manhole covers with a **5-petal flower** + small circles around
**Cyrillic on signs** in these two northern Hokkaido cities
Poles **painted as birch trees**
Huge number of **trekkers/tripods** across many prefectures (see Plonk It's dedicated resource)
